1.synthesis: 'My little children, I am writing these things to you so that you may not sin. But if anyone does sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous. He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world' (vv.1–2). The double test of knowing God: 'And by this we know that we have come to know him, if we keep his commandments. Whoever says "I know him" but does not keep his commandments is a liar, and the truth is not in him, but whoever keeps his word, in him truly the love of God is perfected' (vv.3–5). The criterion: 'whoever says he abides in him ought to walk in the same way in which he walked' (v.6).
1.historical_context: The paraklete title for Christ as heavenly advocate in v.1 reflects legal culture familiar to both Jewish and Greco-Roman audiences. In Roman law, a patronus spoke on behalf of a client before a magistrate; in Jewish legal practice, an intercessor (melits) pleaded the case of the accused. Paul similarly describes Christ's intercession at the right hand of God (Romans 8:34). The hilasmos of v.2 — translated 'propitiation' or 'atoning sacrifice' — is the Greek equivalent of the Hebrew kipper (to atone), the Yom Kippur term. The 'knowing God' boast of v.3 reflects the proto-Gnostic claim to superior gnosis: the secessionists apparently claimed to know God in a way that elevated them beyond ordinary moral obligation. John's counter is behavioral: the test of knowing God is not mystical illumination but commandment-keeping.
1.literary_structure: The section opens with a transitional pastoral moment: John writes so that they may not sin, then immediately provides for the reality of sin — the Advocate who stands before the Father. This is not a contradiction but a pastoral wisdom: the letter that calls to holiness also provides for the community's failure. The hilasmos statement (v.2) is then the ground for the ethical section that follows: because propitiation is already accomplished and extended to the whole world, the response to that accomplishment is obedience, and obedience is the test of knowing. The section closes with the christological criterion of v.6: 'walking as he walked' — the entire ethical section is grounded in the pattern of Christ's own life.
1.theological_application: Gill on 'advocate': the word parakletos means 'one who is called to the side of another' — a legal counselor who appears on behalf of the accused. Christ's advocacy is not a general sentiment of divine goodwill but a specific legal function: he appears before the Father on behalf of the sinning believer, on the basis of his own atoning work. Gill on the 'whole world' in v.2: this does not mean that all people without exception will be saved, but that the propitiation is not restricted to Jewish believers — it extends to Gentiles throughout the world. On v.5, Gill identifies 'the love of God is perfected' as not the achievement of emotional intensity but of purpose: 'God's love in giving his Son, and Christ's love in becoming an advocate and propitiation, are made perfect, effectual, and complete, in those persons who keep the word of God.'
1.christology: Christ is here the simultaneously earthly and heavenly figure: he has already come in the flesh (1:1–4) and is now 'with the Father' as advocate, identified as 'Jesus Christ the righteous.' The righteousness matters: his advocacy is not the pleading of one whose own standing before the Father is uncertain but the intercession of the only righteous one, whose propitiation has settled the sin-debt that the advocate now pleads has been paid. The extension of the propitiation to 'the whole world' (v.2) is the letter's anti-secessionist move in ecclesiological terms: the Docetic splitting of Christ from the human Jesus threatened to restrict salvation to a spiritual elite; John's propitiation-for-the-world insists that the incarnate Son's atoning work is without limit, boundary, or restriction of spiritual class.

7.synthesis: 'Beloved, I am writing you no new commandment, but an old commandment that you had from the beginning. The old commandment is the word that you have heard. At the same time, it is a new commandment that I am writing to you, which is true in him and in you, because the darkness is passing away and the true light is already shining' (vv.7–8). The test in terms of the light/darkness framework: 'Whoever says he is in the light and hates his brother is still in darkness. Whoever loves his brother abides in the light, and in him there is no cause for stumbling. But whoever hates his brother is in the darkness and walks in the darkness, and does not know where he is going, because the darkness has blinded his eyes' (vv.9–11).
7.historical_context: The 'old command' points back to Jesus's own teaching (John 13:34–35: 'A new commandment I give you — love one another') and to the community's founding instruction. The appeal to 'from the beginning' grounds the community's ethic in the original apostolic deposit rather than in any recent revelations claimed by the secessionists, who likely presented their position as a spiritual advance. The 'new commandment' is new in the sense of eschatological newness — the Jeremiah 31 new covenant, the Ezekiel 36 new heart. The light/darkness transition language of v.8 is not Platonic metaphysics but apocalyptic temporal structure: the age of darkness is actually ending and the true light is actually dawning. The stumbling-block (skandalon) language of v.10 echoes OT warnings and early Christian concern for protecting the vulnerable.
7.literary_structure: The section applies the light/darkness framework of ch.1 directly to brotherly love. The paradox of old-yet-new in vv.7–8 is the hinge: the command is old because it comes from Christ's own teaching, new because its eschatological moment has arrived — the light is actually shining, which means the command to love is no longer a preparation for the coming age but the actual life of the age that has begun. Verses 9–11 then test the claim to be 'in the light' against the criterion of love: claiming the light while hating a brother is not merely moral inconsistency but epistemic blindness — the darkness has blinded the eyes of the one who hates, so they cannot even see where they are going.
7.theological_application: Gill on the command as both old and new: 'The commandment of love is not new in this sense, for it was from the beginning of the Gospel ministry of Christ … it is a new one, as it was more clearly explained and enforced by Christ; as it is a new covenant duty and grace; as it is stronger in its obligation upon New Testament saints; and as it is more new and extensive in its objects, even to love enemies.' Matthew Henry observes that the newness is not about what is commanded but about the conditions under which it is now obeyed: the light is shining, the Spirit has been given, the love of God poured out in the heart — the new covenant context makes the old command livable in a way it was not before. The test of v.9 is pastoral in its directness: there is no sophistication available to the person who claims spiritual enlightenment while maintaining hatred of a brother.
7.christology: 'Which is true in him and in you' (v.8) — the new commandment is 'true in him' before it is 'true in you.' Christ's own love is the eschatological reality that makes the new command more than a renewed moral instruction: he himself is the true light that is now shining, the love-in-person whose life is the commandment's content and whose Spirit enables its fulfillment. The 'walking as he walked' of v.6 and the 'true in him' of v.8 make love for the brother a christological act — not the fulfillment of a moral principle but the extension of Christ's own love through those who abide in him.

12.synthesis: John addresses the community in three generational pairs — children, fathers, and young men — to confirm what they already have, before issuing the love-prohibition: 'Do not love the world or the things in the world. If anyone loves the world, the love of the Father is not in him. For all that is in the world — the desires of the flesh and the desires of the eyes and pride of life — is not from the Father but is from the world. And the world is passing away along with its desires, but whoever does the will of God abides forever' (vv.15–17).
12.historical_context: The three-group address (little children, fathers, young men, repeated twice in vv.12–14) may reflect generational stratification corresponding to spiritual maturity — a pattern found in household ethics (1 Tim 5:1–2; Tit 2:1–6). The 'fathers' who 'know him who is from the beginning' are the community's most senior members who have personal continuity with the apostolic witness. The threefold taxonomy of worldly temptation in v.16 — desires of the flesh, desires of the eyes, pride of life — maps onto recognizable Greco-Roman moral categories. The 'world' (kosmos) the letter prohibits loving is not the created order but the social-religious system organized in opposition to God: in Ephesus, this would include the imperial cult, the guild system with its sacrificial requirements, and the cultural prestige of pagan civic life.
12.literary_structure: The six-fold address in vv.12–14 functions as a pastoral inventory: John writes because forgiveness is already secured (v.12), because Christ is known (v.13), because the evil one is overcome (v.13b, v.14c), because the Father is known (v.13b), because the word abides (v.14b). This inventory of spiritual possessions sets up the prohibition of v.15: those who have all of this have no need of the world's substitute offerings. The structure moves from what they are (forgiven, knowing, overcoming) to what they must not do (love the world), and grounds the prohibition eschatologically: the world is passing away, and its desires with it, while the one who does God's will abides forever.
12.theological_application: Gill on 'love not the world': 'The world, or worldly things, are not to be loved in an inordinate manner, so as to be preferred to God and Christ, and divine and spiritual things; for all that is in the world is not from the Father, but from the world.' The prohibition is not asceticism (hatred of material things) but the refusal to allow the world's value-system to displace the Father's love. Gill on v.17: the world's passing is not merely temporal — 'kingdoms, cities, towns, houses, families, estates, and possessions, are continually changing … the pleasures, profits, and honours of the world, are perishing things; they are like a vapour, that appears for a little time, and then vanishes away.' The contrast is permanent: the world and its desires pass, but the one who does the will of God abides forever — the same 'forever' that belongs to the life already manifested in Christ.
12.christology: The six confirmations of vv.12–14 are all grounded in relation to Christ: forgiveness is 'for his name's sake' (v.12), the fathers know 'him who is from the beginning' (v.13 — the same language as the prologue), the young men have overcome the evil one. The forgiveness, the knowledge, and the victory are all christological: they do not belong to the community independently but through the one whose name secures forgiveness, whose eternity grounds the fathers' knowledge, and whose cross defeated the evil one. The world-love prohibition follows necessarily: those whose forgiveness, knowledge, and victory are all located in Christ have no need of what the world offers — its alternatives are inferior substitutes for what they already fully possess.

18.synthesis: 'Children, it is the last hour, and as you have heard that antichrist is coming, so now many antichrists have come. Therefore we know that it is the last hour. They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would have continued with us' (vv.18–19). But the community has the anointing: 'But you have been anointed by the Holy One, and you all have knowledge' (v.20). The defining lie: 'Who is the liar but he who denies that Jesus is the Christ? This is the antichrist, he who denies the Father and the Son. No one who denies the Son has the Father. Whoever confesses the Son has the Father also' (vv.22–23).
18.historical_context: The 'antichrist' figure combines Jewish expectations of an eschatological adversary (Belial in Qumran, the man of lawlessness in 2 Thess 2) with the immediate historical crisis of secessionists departing the community. Their departure is interpreted theologically rather than sociologically: 'they went out from us, but they were not of us' (v.19) — those who leave the community reveal that they never belonged to it in the deepest sense. The Cerinthian Christology that lies behind v.22 separated the earthly Jesus from the heavenly Christ: Cerinthus taught (according to Irenaeus) that Christ descended on Jesus at baptism and departed before the crucifixion. To 'deny that Jesus is the Christ' in this context means to sever the human name from the divine title — precisely what the letter's eyewitness prologue refutes. The chrisma (anointing) of v.20 is John's counter to the secessionists' claimed superior gnosis.
18.literary_structure: The section moves from the eschatological time-marker ('last hour', v.18) through the community crisis (the antichrists who have gone out, vv.18–19) to the community's resource (the anointing of the Holy One, v.20) to the defining theological error (the denial of the Son, v.22) and its consequence (losing the Father also, v.23). The inclusion of v.19b — 'that it might become plain that they all are not of us' — frames the departure eschatologically: the secessionist crisis is not a pastoral failure but a revelatory event, making visible who was and was not genuinely of the community. The anointing is the community's prophylactic against the antichrist error: because the Holy One has anointed them, they know the truth and can recognize the lie.
18.theological_application: Augustine in his homilies addresses the 'last hour' directly: 'In this lesson he addresses the children that they may make haste to grow, because "it is the last hour." … No man is "born of water and the Spirit," except he be willing. Consequently if he will, he grows.' The eschatological urgency is not occasion for panic but for growth — the last hour is the hour in which growth toward full maturity matters most. Gill on v.22: 'He that denies that Jesus of Nazareth is the Messiah of the prophets, is not indeed the only liar in the world, but he is the greatest of liars; this is a consummate lie, being opposed to a glaring truth, to a fact clear and indisputable.' The theological stakes of the Christological denial are not merely doctrinal but relational — to deny the Son is to lose the Father, because the only access to the Father is through the Son whom the Docetists were dismantling.
18.christology: The letter's sharpest christological statement in this section is the inseparability of Father and Son: 'No one who denies the Son has the Father. Whoever confesses the Son has the Father also' (v.23). This is the theological logic behind the letter's anti-Docetic polemic: the Docetic or Cerinthian separation of the human Jesus from the divine Christ does not preserve access to the divine — it severs it. If the Son who was made flesh is not genuinely the Son, then the Father who sent him has not genuinely revealed himself, and there is no access to the Father through a figure who is something other than the Son. The confession that the human Jesus is the Christ — the one anointed — is the only gateway to the Father, because the Father and Son are inseparable in their mutual disclosure.

24.pericope_label: Continue in the Son and the Father
24.synthesis: 'Let what you heard from the beginning abide in you. If what you heard from the beginning abides in you, then you too will abide in the Son and in the Father. And this is the promise that he made to us — eternal life' (vv.24–25). The anointing is sufficient: 'The anointing that you received from him abides in you, and you have no need that anyone should teach you … his anointing teaches you about everything, and is true, and is no lie — just as it has taught you, abide in him' (v.27). The eschatological exhortation: 'And now, little children, abide in him, so that when he appears we may have confidence and not shrink from him in shame at his coming' (v.28).
24.historical_context: The 'remain in' (menein en) language is characteristically Johannine (John 15:4–7; 17:21–23): the mutual abiding of the community in Christ and Christ in the community. The condition for this abiding is that 'what you heard from the beginning' — the original apostolic proclamation about the incarnate Son — continues to hold the community. The claim that the anointing 'teaches you about everything' and that the community has 'no need that anyone should teach you' (v.27) must be read against Jeremiah 31:33–34's new covenant promise: 'I will put my law in their minds … No longer will they teach their neighbor … because they will all know me.' The new covenant fulfillment means the community does not need a supplementary teaching to the apostolic proclamation — the Spirit's anointing is sufficient. The parousia confidence/shame language of v.28 reflects Mediterranean honor-shame culture: the moment of Christ's appearing is the public moment of vindication or disgrace.
24.literary_structure: The section closes ch.2 with a double movement: the community is called to hold fast to what it already has (the apostolic message from the beginning, v.24) and to abide in the one it already knows (the Son, v.28). The anointing (v.27) provides the internal resource for this holding fast: the Spirit who anoints does not add new content but confirms and deepens the community's apprehension of the content already received. The parousia of v.28 gives the abiding an eschatological horizon: the present act of abiding is preparation for the public moment of Christ's appearing, which will be the moment of either confidence or shame depending on whether the community has held its christological confession.
24.theological_application: Gill on v.28: 'The apostle having finished his separate instructions exhortations to the fathers, young men and children, returns to the whole body of the saints … and whom he exhorts to abide in Christ, that is, in the exercise of faith on him, of hope in him, and love to him; and to hold to him the head, and to hold fast his word and Gospel, and to continue in the truth and doctrine of him.' The abiding is not a passive state but an active holding — faith, hope, love, and confessional fidelity all constitute the community's abiding. Augustine on the same verse grounds the community's confidence at the parousia in the same love that has been the letter's criterion: those who have loved will have confidence; those who have hated their brother will have cause for shame. The eschatological horizon reveals what the ethics of the letter have been preparing for.
24.christology: The promise of v.25 — 'this is the promise that he made to us — eternal life' — connects directly to the letter's opening: the eternal life that was with the Father and was made manifest is now the life promised to those who abide in the Son. The eternal life is not a future state detached from present relationship but the content of the Son himself: to abide in him is to already be in possession of the eternal life he is, even while awaiting its full manifestation at his appearing. The parousia of v.28 is not the arrival of something alien to present experience but the public disclosure of what the community already has in hidden form — the life that is in the Son, experienced now through the Spirit's anointing, revealed then in the glory of his coming.
